title: Non-Hermitian Landau Levels
abstract: We formulate non-Hermitian Landau levels in two-dimensional systems under a complex perpendicular magnetic field. In the symmetric gauge, we derive their discretely spaced, highly degenerate complex spectra and biorthogonal eigenstates, and clarify the role of non-unitary gauge transformations. A non-Hermitian Harper-Hofstadter lattice model confirms the continuum theory and reveals Gaussian wave packet dynamics governed by semiclassical equations with a complex Lorentz force, pointing to possible experimental realizations of complex magnetic fields.
summary: This paper develops the theory of Landau levels in the presence of a complex-valued magnetic field. It demonstrates that such fields lead to complex energy spectra and spiral-shaped particle trajectories. The findings suggest new ways to implement non-Hermitian physics in platforms like topoelectrical circuits and ultracold atoms.
main_problem: The paper investigates the properties, topology, and dynamics of Landau levels in two-dimensional systems subject to a complex-valued perpendicular magnetic field.
main_result: The authors derive complex-valued, highly degenerate energy spectra and show that the imaginary part of the magnetic field induces a drag force, turning circular cyclotron motion into spiral trajectories.
method: The study uses an algebraic approach with creation and annihilation operators, a biorthogonal formalism for non-Hermitian eigenstates, and numerical simulations of a non-Hermitian Harper-Hofstadter lattice model.
model_or_system: A 2D system of charged particles in a complex magnetic field, modeled both in the continuum using a non-Hermitian Hamiltonian and on a square lattice via a non-Hermitian Harper-Hofstadter model.
key_observables: Complex eigenenergies, biorthogonal eigenstates, wave packet center-of-mass trajectories, and effective mass.
important_parameters: Complex magnetic field B = Re(B) + iIm(B), cyclotron frequency, magnetic length, and wave packet width.
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes a semiclassical framework for wave packet dynamics and requires Re(B) > |Im(B)| for wavefunction square-integrability in the Landau gauge.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows complex eigenenergies and numerical right eigenstates; Figure 2 displays wave packet trajectories (spiraling motion); Figure 3 shows effective mass as a function of wave packet width.
paper_structure: The paper begins with the formulation of the continuum theory and algebraic derivation of complex Landau levels, moves to the analysis of gauge dependence and localization, then transitions to lattice model simulations and semiclassical wave packet dynamics, and concludes with an investigation of effective mass dependence.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant for researchers in open quantum systems and many-body dynamics as it provides a fundamental framework for understanding how non-Hermiticity (via complex magnetic fields) alters topological features and transport properties like the Lorentz force.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. Eigenvalues and right eigenstates of the non-Hermitian Harper-Hofstadter model on a 50 × 50 square lattice with lattice spacing a = 1 and the complex-valued magnetic field B = 0.1−0.001i. In panel (a) the complex-valued eigenenergies are plotted, where the color indicates the planar angular momentum calculated from the eigenstates. The first ten non-Hermitian Landau levels can be identified from the eigenvalue distribution. The inset shows that for larger m the numerical eigenenergy deviates from the analytical value as described in the text. To prove that this is indeed a finite-size effect we show the numerical eigenstates from three states from the lowest non-Hermitian Landau...

Figure 2. Trajectories of the center of mass of Gaussian wave packets in presence of a complex-valued magnetic field. Their evolution is simulated on a 50 × 50 square lattice governed by the Harper-Hofstadter model with B = 0.1 −0.001i in the symmetric gauge with centered origin. The initial position is marked by a black circle. In panel (a) the spiral trajectory of a wave packet with finite initial velocity is shown in blue and compared to the solution of the semi-classical equation of mo- tion, plotted in red. Figure 3. Effective mass meff depending on wave packet width for a 50 × 50 square lattice for vanishing magnetic field.
